tokens: Apollo 8 has reached another historic milestone in its voyage as its crew of Frank Borman, Jim Lovell and Bill Anders become the first humans to be captured by an gravitational field other than Earth's.
Having passed behind the Moon, they fired the spacecraft's large engine for a little over four minutes, slowing them down enough to stay in lunar orbit.
The manoeuvre complete, they have just reappeared to the antennae of Earth and a highly relieved Mission Control are talking to them and looking at the telemetry that the spacecraft is sending down.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We have a crew report of an orbit of 60.5 nautical miles by 169 nautical miles [112 by 313 kilometres].
Standing by, continuing to monitor; this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Verb 64 is called up on the DSKY (Display and Keyboard) which brings up a routine to operate their High Gain Antenna and properly acquire a link with Earth.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Jim doesn't think that Houston is reading them yet.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Verify your evaporator water control [is] in Automatic.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Environmental Control System experts in the back room at Mission Control are noticing that the cooling radiator temperatures are higher than normal.
Normally, this would cause a water evaporator system to come online, and provide additional cooling by the sublimation of water.
Houston suspects that the system has either "dried out", or is manual mode.
Normally, the system is in Automatic mode, and support crews need to confirm this.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: A 16-mm movie camera is aimed, we believe, out the left-hand rendezvous window.
The Flight Plan calls for a frame rate of one frame per second.
When replayed on Earth usually at the standard 24 frames-per-second, the effect will be to apparently speed up the motion across the lunar surface.
One reason for the slow rate is to maximise the capture of photographic data with the minimum expenditure of scarce film.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We read you loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Reading you loud and clear now.
And verify your evaporator water control panel switch to the Auto position.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Though the transcript mentions the DSKY, this may be a reference to one of the meters on panel 2 that monitors the spacecraft's cooling systems.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: As will become apparent, theevaporator is prone to drying out.
At this time of high activity in the spacecraft, many systems have been powered up for the LOI burn and the evaporator ought to be helping to remove the extra heat generated.
If it has dried out, we would expect the temperatures at its outlet to rise.
Bill will have to service the evaporator to restore its function.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Burn status report as follows: Burn on time.
Burn time 4 minutes, 6½ seconds; VGX, minus 1.4; attitudes nominal, no trim, VGYwas zero, VGZwas plus 0.2, Delta-VCwas minus 20.2.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: water control on panel 382 is Auto.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: On the left-hand side of the spacecraft is a blank panel that covers panel 382.
Behind that are controls for the ECS (Environmental Control System).
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The two controls near the bottom are those for the evaporators.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Do you recommend activating the secondary water boiler?
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: The conversation taking place is with Bill Anders aboard the spacecraft.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The crew tend to refer to the evaporators as 'boilers' because that is essentially what they do.
Readers should be aware that this does not mean they operate at 100°C.
The vacuum in which they operate means that the water boils at a low temperature, cold enough to form ice.
This was a worry in the Command Module evaporators because if the water supply were to ice up, it could crack the pipes within the ECS unit and breach the spacecraft's pressure integrity.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Re-verify manual valve on panel 382, Evaporator water control, Automatic.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Recommend you activate your secondary water evaporator.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Since the primary water evaporator isn't working, Houston is recommending that the secondary cooling system be turned on.
This will certainly take care of their immediate cooling problems, but the long term solution is to get the primary evaporator working again.
The secondary system has it's own evaporator which is entirely automatic.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Our ground data closely coincides with that aboard the spacecraft.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Turn off your DSE and we'll go to high bit rate.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: I'll continue my readback of the burn status report.
Copied VGX, zero; VGY, zero; VGZ, 1.2; Delta-V Charlie, minus 20.2.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Stand by; he's getting the chart out again.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: We're on malfunction 1 of 6, going through step 1 to step 2.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: What you're hearing here is checkout procedure of the Environmental Control System.
The voice principally from the spacecraft, that of Bill Anders, the systems engineer member of the team aboard.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Looks like the boiler dried out somewhere along the line.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: Ground crews had noticed the tendency of this evaporator to dry out.
Their conclusion, stated during the Flight Readiness Review of 19 November 1968 was that it wasn't a problem because the crew could always reservice the unit.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Our ground readings on this orbit; 168 nautical miles in apolune, perilune of 60.4 nautical miles [311.1 by 111.9 km].
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This determination of their orbit made by analysis of their range and Doppler effect as measured by the Earth stations agrees very closely with the on-board solution.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I'd like to confirm that burn status report.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: The burn was on time, 4 minutes and 6½ seconds; VGX, minus 1.4; trim nominal; VGY, 0; VGZ, minus 0.2; Delta-V Charlie, minus 20.2.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: We copy your apogee and perigee.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Bill is looking at the pressure generated by the water evaporation process.
Technically, it is correct to say that the water vapor created is "steam", but this is not the hot, 100 degree C we think of when we say steam.
Rather, it is cold water vapor, under very slight pressure (under 0.2 psi).
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: So you've had the first status report from an Apollo crew in lunar orbit.
The unmanned Lunar Orbiter spacecraft traversed the Moon, perhaps over 10,000 times, but this is the first that man aboard, in this case Frank Borman, reported to his compatriots here on Earth.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: At times when the crew are not talking directly to Earth, their onboard conversation can be heard breaking through onto the comm circuit.
Though highly distorted, occasional words can sometimes be discerned, Bill's reply here being an example.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, keep a good eye on it.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: Tell Ken, nice job on the malfunction procedures.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: Give us a call when you think we ought to stop the secondary boiler, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: For information, we're passing over just to the side of the crater Langrenus at this time, going into the Sea of Fertility.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: As you heard, Apollo 8 approaching the Sea of Fertility.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Langrenus is a striking, large crater on the southeastern shore of Mare Fecunditatis (Sea of Fertility).
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This photo, taken by the author (Woods), shows Langrenus with the Sun shining from the west.
This low lighting shows the complex topography of the crater.
Being sited near the eastern limb of the Moon, for Apollo 8, the Sun is already quite high over Langrenus, making its topography more difficult to distinguish.
It is 132 kilometres in diameter, has a collection of central peaks and a complex system of terraces inside its rim, the result of large-scale slumping of its walls soon after its formation.
The crater carries the latinised name of Michel Florent van Langren (circa 1600 - 1675), a Flemish mapmaker who pioneered the production of lunar maps.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Our first batch of ground tracking data shows agreement in velocity within one foot per second with that of the spacecraft.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: What does the ole Moon look like from 60 miles?
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: The Moon is essentially gray, no color; looks like plaster of Paris or sort of a grayish beach sand.
We can see quite a bit of detail.
The Sea of Fertility doesn't stand out as well here as it does back on Earth.
There's not as much contrast between that and the surrounding craters.
[Pause.] The craters are all rounded off.
There's quite a few of them, some of them are newer.
[Pause.] Many of them look like - especially the round ones - look like hit by meteorites or projectiles of some sort.
[Pause.] Langrenus is quite a huge crater; it's got a central cone to it.
[Long pause.] The walls of the crater are terraced, about six or seven different terraces on the way down.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Jim's impressions of what he sees have been replayed many times on documentary films of the Apollo program, especially his first sentence.

Though his words are honest, they predate NASA's emphasis on science and geology that led to later commanders, Jim included, becoming accomplished field geologists themselves and enthusiasts of what the Moon's landscape has to offer.
Perhaps as a consequence, his portrayal of the lunar surface as grey and colourless set a tone for the public's subsequent perception of the Moon as uninteresting.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And coming up now in the Sea of Fertility are the old friends Messier and Pickering that I looked about so much on Earth.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Crater Pickering has since been renamed Messier A.
It and Messier are a distinctive pair of craters in the middle of Mare Fecunditatis.
Both are elongated with their long axis roughly aligned.
A striking pair of rays splay across the mare surface to the west while fainter rays are cast north and south at right angles to these.
The curious geometry of this pair have led to a range of formation scenarios, the most popular of which invokes a bouncing impactor.
This holds that an object hit at an extremely shallow angle, travelling east to west.
It gouged out Messier, flew a further 25 kilometres before forming Messier A.
The craters are named after Charles Messier (1730-1817) who was a prolific discoverer of comets.
To aid his work, he compiled a catalogue of fuzzy celestial objects for which he is now far better known.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And I can see the rays coming out of blaze [?] Pickering.
We're coming up now near our P-1 initial site which I'm going to try and see.
Be advised the round window, the hatch window, is completely iced over; we cannot use it.
Bill and I are sharing the rendezvous window.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: The hatch window is not literally iced over.
Rather, an outgassing of fumes from the sealant materials around the large windows is fogging them up.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: One of the tasks assigned to Apollo 8 is to take a close look at one of the favoured sites for a future Apollo landing.
Apollo site selection was, by this time, constraining the number of suitable sites to just five, two of which were smooth areas to the east of the Moon's near side.

The easternmost site, east of the crater Maskelyne and near the southern shore of Mare Tranquillitatis, is the prime target for Apollo 8 even to the extent that the time of launch from Earth was chosen so that the lighting at the site would be similar to that expected on a real landing mission.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The second site in Mare Tranquillitatis was originally selected to be 12° further west from P-1, this being the distance the Moon's terminator moves in an Earth-day.
Then, if the launch of a landing mission had to be postponed for a day, the lighting conditions would be repeated further west.
In seven months, Apollo 11 would land at this second, more westerly site.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Got any more information on those rays?
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: The rays out of Pickering are quite faint from here; there are two different groups coming - going to the left, they don't appear to be - have any depth to them at all, just rays coming out.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: They look like just changes in the color of the mare.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: The rays coming from the Messier craters, like most rays on the Moon, are caused by shock-fractured rock being sprayed out over the mare.
The fracturing exposes crystalline surfaces that catch the sunlight.
Lovell seems to have been looking out for the rays adding a hummocky topography to the mare surface.
Instead, he observes that they add only a thin veneer.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Bill, if you can tear yourself away from that window, we'd like you to turn off the secondary evaporator.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Coolant temperatures have now lowered to a point where the backup radiators are no longer needed.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You can leave that secondary pump on for just a few minutes.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Over to my right are the Pyrenee Mountains coming up and we're just about over Messier and Pickering [Messier A] right now.
Our first initial point is easily seen from our altitude.
We're getting quite a bit of contrast as we appear - as we approach the terminator.
[Pause.] The view appears to be good, no reflection of the Sun back to our eyes; it appears that visibility at this particular spot is excellent.
It's very easy to pick out our first initial point; and over this mountain chain we can see the second initial point, the Triangular Mountain.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Jim is looking towards the southern side of their current ground track, and as they pass across the centre of Mare Fecunditatis, he can see the mountain range that borders the eastern shore of Mare Nectaris.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The approaches to the landing sites included a couple of recognisable points the crews could use to check the timings of a landing trajectory.
Jim has just called the second one of these the Triangular Mountain but later in the mission, he will label it Mount Marilyn after his wife.

Though not official for many years, and having been included in the storyline of the 1995 movieApollo 13., Mount Marilyn was adopted by the International Astronautical Union in 2017 as the name for this triangular formation.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Now we're coming upon the craters Colombo and Gutenberg.
We can see the long parallel faults or grabens.
[Pause.] And they run through the mare material right into the highland material.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: The principal speaker that you've heard during most of this discourse has been Jim Lovell but that last voice was that of Bill Anders.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The Mission Report includes a list of all the Targets of Opportunity that Bill has been tasked to photograph if possible.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Target of Opportunity 72 is a cluster of craters east of the crater Colombo, or to its left in this photograph composited from AS08-13-2215 to 2227 using low-resolution index scans.
Colombo and Gutenberg are two large craters between Mare Fecunditatis and Montes Pyrenaeus.
Also of note in this area is Goclenius.
Note that perspective effects mean that the top and bottom of this pan will have large geometric distortions.
The target is the cluster of craters beyond and including Crozier.
These craters all have a similar size yet have different floor morphologies.
To the right of Crozier is one of only a handful of "doughnut craters" on the Moon.
It is far from clear how these unusual crater-within-a-crater features formed.
They all have a similar shape and size and both the outer and inner crater seem to have been formed together.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This image, AS08-13-2225, is part of the sequence in the earlier composite image.
Note the impressive rille system can be seen within Goclenius.
There are many of these large 'fractured floor' craters on the Moon but Goclenius stands out because it also straddles a set of graben-type rilles that border Mare Fecunditatis.
By the fact that at least one of these rilles is superimposed on the crater floor, rim and surrounding mare shows how relative ages can be determined from this photograph.
The crater itself is the earliest feature here followed by the smooth mare surface that surrounds it and laps against its exterior wall.
The interior of Goclenius and the rilles that cross its surface probably also formed around this time.
Finally, the immense weight of the dense basalts of Mare Fecunditatis caused the centre of the mare to sink.
This stretched the landscape at the shore, forming the large arcuate grabens that now cut across Goclenius.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Goclenius is the latinised name of Rudolf Gockel (1572-1621), a German scientist.
The crater, Colombo is named after the seafarer, Christopher Columbus (1451-1506) who discovered America in 1492.
Johann Gutenburg (1398-1468) was the German inventor of movable type and the printing press, and Francis Crozier (1796-1848) was a polar explorer.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We're directly over our first initial point now for B-1.
It's almost impossible to miss, very easy to pick out and we can look right over into the second initial point.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: The Flight Plan gives coordinates for B-1 as 2.675° north, 35.025° east.
This is located on the eastern side of Mare Tranquillitatis.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I can see very clearly the five crater star formation which we had on our lunar charts.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: And right now, I'm trying to pick out visually B-1.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Bill, you can turn off the secondary Evap.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: How about giving us a system status, please?
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: I've got B-1 in sight now, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: The reference to B-1 is a landmark, a landmark which relates to a projected landing site.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: You can see the entire rims of the craters from here with, of course, the white crescent on the far side were the Sun is shining on it.
The shadows are quite lengthy now.
Maskelyne B has quite a few shadows off of it, but could be recognized.
Just to the west of Maskelyne B, we start going to the terminator.
The terminator is actually quite sharp over the Pyrenees, and it's - I can't see anything in Earthshine at this present time.
Bill says that he can see things out the side window since he's not looking down on sunshine on the Moon.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Maskelyne B is a 9.2-km crater about 20 km west of Maskelyne itself.

At this time it is virtually on the terminator and since it is about 6° west of Apollo Landing Site 1 (P-1), the landing site is illuminated with a 6° Sun, close to what would be expected on a landing mission.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: As a matter of interest, spacecraft Commander Frank Borman's heart rate has been ranging between 78 and 80 since we acquired.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're evaluating the strip charts on your SPS burn and we'll give you a read-out on that shortly.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: In today's world of real-time computer displays, it is often surprising that data in Apollo was usually recorded with pens drawing traces on long strips of paper.

Rather than seeming archaic, this was considered a reasonable way of recording data, which allowed engineers to look at data in real-time and even mark up points of interest, as well as making a permanent record of the data.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Do you need high bit rate anymore?
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: We have dumped your DSE, and we'd like to stick with high bit rate for a while.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Ground controllers still want to take a look at the data in more detail.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Well, we're just about over Maskelyne B now, and our target is just directly below us.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: If you want the recorder now, it's yours.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Our tracking data from the ground still compares very well with the guidance and navigation computer on the spacecraft.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Target of Opportunity 90 is a 49-km crater Capella within the Montes Pyrenaeum range for which Bill took frames AS08-13-2228 to AS08-13-2237.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Most of these frames have been composited together to produce the above montage.
The view looks across the terminator towards Capella.
The low lighting shows the topography of the area well, especially the raised rims of the craters and the lines of the various rilles that make up Rimae Gutenburg.
The crater was named after a fifth century lawyer who postulated that Venus and Mercury orbited the Sun.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Bill finishes his photography with mag E on this rev with three frames.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The near wall of Fracastorius, a large, 124-km walled plain, is missing and the lavas within open out onto Nectaris.
Being right on the terminator, only its rim is illuminated.
The spacecraft is now flying over the night-time side of the Moon though they are still in sunlight.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: MSFN [Manned Spaceflight Tracking Network] tracking is comparing very well with your onboard nav.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: ...we lost radio contact at the exact second you predicted.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: The reference there was to Loss of Signal as they went over the back side of the Moon.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Are you sure you didn't turn off the transmitters at that time?
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Frank had been amazed at the accuracy of the predictions by Mission Control, eliciting a joke from Bill that their boss had probably ordered the signal to be cut so as not to worry the crew if there was a slight error.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: While these other guys are all looking at the Moon, I want to make sure we got a good SPS.
How about giving me that report when you can?
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: And we want a Go for every rev, please; or otherwise, we'll burn in TEI-1 at your direction.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Writer Robert Zimmerman, in his book 'Genesis: The Story of Apollo 8', ascribes Frank's exhortation to a desire to ensure Mission Control are concentrating on the state of the spacecraft and the mission.
Frank will return to Earth if there is any reason to doubt the integrity of CSM-103 and Mission Control must actively agree to allow each orbit to commence.
There is no doubt who is in control of the spacecraft.
TEI-1 is the next opportunity to return to Earth, and there is less than one hour to LOS.
Frank is exercising his prerogative as commander of the mission to come home early if the spacecraft is not in perfect shape.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Bill is providing a running commentary of his photographic work on the voice recorder that was presumably meant to help researchers determine the context of the images.
It certainly helps us in correlating the photographic index with the historic record.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The photographic index shows the first 29 frames of magazine A were used to take pictures of Earth or the S-IVB third stage of the Saturn V during their outward coast.
There was one dark frame, then six frames including four of the 132-km crater Langrenus.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: When you go into the dark in about 7 or 8 minutes, I have some words for you on the filters for the wide-angle lens, for your TV camera.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Despite Borman's comment about being in the dark already, they are still in sunlight.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Let me know when you're ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Jim is carrying out another realignment of the spacecraft's guidance platform, known as a P52 after the program used for the operation.
Capella is one of two stars Jim is sighting on as part of the realignment, the other being Regulus, the dominant star in Leo.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: It is planned to complete one of these realignments during the dark pass of every orbit in case the crew have to leave the Moon's vicinity in a hurry.
That way they will have an accurately aligned platform ready for the subsequent burn of their SPS (Service Propulsion System) engine.

During coasting flight to and from the Moon, realignments are carried out at about 8 hour intervals and it is interesting to note that the figures given for platform drift are roughly the same for 2-hour or 8-hour intervals.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Earth - Earthshine is about as expected, Houston.
Not as much detail, of course, as in the sunlight, but you can see the large craters quite distinctly, and you can see the albedo contacts quite distinctly.
And, also, the - there's a good three-dimensional view of the rims of the larger craters.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: I think our high-speed film will be able to pick some of this stuff up quite well.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: Jim's sighting on the stars has been very accurate as the computer has displayed five zeros (five balls) as being the star angle difference.
In other words, the angle Jim measured between the two selected stars is identical to the known angle between them.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Jim has also read out the torquing angles from the P52.
The gimbals that support the platform must be moved through these angles to restore perfect alignment.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Bill is making a check of the spacecraft's systems per the note in his column of the Flight Plan at 070:14.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: As Apollo 8 passes over the night portion of the Moon, the guidance and navigation - the platform is to be aligned.
This during periods of darkness, since the spacecraft remains in an inertially fixed attitude for this procedure.
This leaves lunar daylight periods for maneuverability needed for photography and visual observations.
At 70 hours, 12 minutes, continuing to monitor; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Go ahead with your information on the filter, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: We recommend you use a wide-angle lens on this particular TV run.
You can use the telephoto lens with the same setup as yesterday's TV show.
However, we recommend a wide-angle lens.
Step number 1, tape the single red filter to the red filter on the red/blue filter holder; do it so that the filter slide still functions.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Based on the bright results they had with an earlier TV transmission, it seems likely the red filter is being added simply to reduce the light levels going into the lens.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Step number 2, attach the filter holder to the lens with tape on the top and bottom.
Do this with the slide forward.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Then at the end of your second rev TV pass, or on request from here, we'd like you to remove that red filter from the holder and transmit briefly with it that way, then slide it over the blue side for your final transmission.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: About now, Apollo 8 moves into the Moon's shadow, to reappear at about 71 hours GET.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The telephoto lens has a narrow, 9° field of view.
This makes it particularly difficult to aim accurately at an object that is also photographically significant.
With the wide-angle lens, a large vista can be imaged for the audiences on Earth.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Standing by to record TEI-1 and TEI-2.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Your TEI-1 and -2 PADs you received last pass are still good.
Using these PADs, your next midcourse would be less than 20 feet per second.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: At 067:35:29, before they entered lunar orbit, two PADs were read up that included the information that would allow the crew to return home after the first and second orbits have been completed.
These are the TEI-1 and -2 PADs and would only be used in the event of an emergency.
Their burn to enter lunar orbit is close enough to what was planned that the original TEI abort PADs are still valid.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We have all of the SPS experts looking at your data now.
The preliminary look is very good, and we'll give you some final words later.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: We could feel the chug when we threw in bank B - not a chug, but we could feel the additional thrust.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: The SPS engine has two independent systems for injecting propellants into the combustion chamber.
The A bank was brought in first while the B bank was manually started a few seconds later.
The engine does not achieve its full thrust until both banks are operating.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, be advised on this red/blue filter technique on the TV.
You cannot slide the two filters out of the way with them taped onto the TV camera.
So I suggest we do red, blue, and then take them off.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: We concur, but make sure that the little red filter is taped over the big one.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Verb 83 brings up numbers on the DSKY relating to parameters used during rendezvous, something that will not be happening on this mission.
In this context, one number in particular is of interest as it gives the crew their angle relative to the local horizontal, known as theta.
This is required to give the ORDEAL an appropriate starting point for displaying their local attitude on the FDAI (Flight Director Attitude Indicator) or "8-ball".
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You don't want the red fil - you want the blue by itself.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: Bill, we'd like you to use the double red filter for the first transmission.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: If you should decide that you want to roll heads up on rev 2, one thing to remember, be sure you yaw 45 degrees right in order to maintain your High Gain Antenna comm.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: We will not do that; we're going to stick with the Flight Plan and make the best we can here.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: As usual, in the real world, the Flight Plan looks a lot fuller than it did in Florida.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Out on the end of it's arm, the HGA (High Gain Antenna) has a limited range of articulation, and the Flight Plan was carefully designed to allow it to maintain a link with Earth during lunar manoeuvres.
In particular, if the crew are in a "heads-down" attitude, the windows will be facing the surface while the HGA, on the opposite side of the spacecraft, will have easy access to Earth.

With the fogged windows affecting visibility, there is a possibility the crew might have wanted to adopt a "heads-up" attitude which would have brought the HGA around to the Moon's side of the spacecraft.
Mission Control are reminding the crew that to keep the HGA pointing at Earth in this attitude, they would need to yaw to the right.
However, Frank is for minimising changes to their packed schedule.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: A period of relative quiet; perhaps the crew has decided to start their first meal in lunar orbit.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The GDC (Gyro Display Coupler) provides a backup attitude reference with information coming from the Gyro Assemblies.

By the nature of this system, it is more prone to drift than the gyro system in the IMU, so occasionally, it's knowledge of the spacecraft attitude is updated with that from the IMU by pressing the GDC Align button.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Having held an inertial attitude since they entered lunar orbit, they will begin an orb-rate rotation to keep one side of the spacecraft aimed at the Moon.
An initial attitude for this rotation is given in the Flight Plan though the figures given by Jim differ in somewhat in pitch.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: After Apollo 8 reaches the sunlit side of the Moon, Jim is scheduled to carry out a series of sightings through the hatch window of various control points.
Of course, the hatch window is fogged over to the point of being useless.
We can assume that they are changing their attitude so that the rendezvous windows can be used.
Here, Bill refers to the DSKY as a "keypunch".
The phrase "keypunch" reflects the ubiquitous way data was entered into computers of the day.
Personal computers certainly didn't exist, nor did even the "dumb terminals" that characterized computers in the 1970's.
Cards had holes punched onto them that represented data, using machines like the IBM 029 keypunch machine.
One of the editors (O'Brien) remembers fondly (yeah, right) of the hours spent keypunching programs on the 029.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We're now less than 30 minutes away from our LOS time on this, the first revolution in lunar orbit.
Continuing to monitor; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Jerry Carr is referring to the O2purge of the fuel cells, used to clear out impurities in the system.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And we're standing by for a map update.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: Just for information, after we completed P52, I've acquired the Earth in the sextant.
It's quite a sight from here.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: How are the systems experts on the SPS coming, Jerry?
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: After emerging from the Moon's eastern limb, engineering data from the spacecraft, including the data from the LOI burn, was replayed to Earth.
Once gathered at the Earth station, it has to be passed on to Houston before analysis of the engine's performance can begin.
Frank is especially keen to know whether it performed well as they need it to get back home.
It has been 15 minutes since Frank's original request for a review of SPS data, and it is 30 minutes until LOS.
Frank is likely getting a bit impatient about the time it is taking to get an answer about an update on the status of the engine.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: They're still working, Frank; another five or ten minutes.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Your SPS data; looking real good.
It is just a matter of getting it all in from the site and getting it looked at.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: We've just received data from our Flight Surgeon that Frank Borman's peak heart rate at LOI-1 read 130.
The same reading that he had, as a matter of fact, at lift off.
Thought we would pass that along.
Continuing to monitor, this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We'd like to take about five minutes of high bit rate.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Five minutes of high bit rate coming on.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Apollo 8, Houston, with a map update.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Go ahead with the map update.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: Pages 1-18 to 1-20 of the Flight Plan have forms for the crew to take note of these timings.
They represent when the spacecraft passes significant points during its orbit and help the crew keep track of when events occur.
Though the Flight Plan has many of these times marked, they are only approximate.
These times are based on the spacecraft's measured orbit and are accurate.
The map update is interpreted as follows:Loss of Signal (LOS) before start of rev 2:073:04:57.
This is the time they lose contact with Earth as they go behind the Moon for the second time.Spacecraft sunrise in orbit:073:09:37.

After LOS, the spacecraft returns to sunlight, crossing the Moon's sunset terminator soon after.Passing over meridian of 150°W:073:19:01.Acquisition Of Signal (AOS):073:48:53.Spacecraft sunset in orbit:074:24:23.
At this time, Apollo 8 will pass from lunar daytime into the lunar night a short time after passing over the sunrise terminator.Landmark Bravo-1:074:16:24.
The orbital evaluation of Apollo Landing Site (ALS)-1, known as B-1, is a major goal of the mission.
This is a timing for its acquisition.This map update is relevant to the second and third revolutions around the Moon.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We show you 23 minutes to LOS.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: And are you going to dump the tape?
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: SPS evaluation is still underway and looking good.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: We're still using the tape recorder.
We'll dump it in a little bit.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: You just heard that Go for Rev 2.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: You can go to low bit rate.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Flight Director Glynn Lunney crossed checked with his EECOM and Guidance and Control Officers, and told our Capsule Communicator, Jerry Carr, to pass along that Go for Rev 2.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Put your Telemetry Input switch to Low.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: We're in the process of preparing meal 4, day - correction - day 4, meal A.
transcript.speaker: Borman

tokens: To try to improve intelligibility, a version with some noise reduction applied is included.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Are you going to be able to dump that tape prior to LOS?
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: Bill, they say they've already dumped the tape, and it's almost totally clean.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: That means you have got about 2 minutes of low bit rate on there, but the rest is clean.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: The high bit rate of the burn wasn't on there?
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: We've already dumped and got that.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Let me know when you're going to dump it next time, Jerry.
I understand we're Go now on the DSE.
Have you got any voice off of it?
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: The voice quality on your tape was just fair to middling.
We were able to monitor your burn and hear most of that pretty well.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Did you get a report of the photography accomplished, or is that on the tape at present?
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: We'll put it on the tape now.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: Having learned that the DSE tape has been dumped to Earth, Bill then uses it to leave a note of the progress of his photography.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Now less than 5 minutes away from Loss of Signal on our first revolution.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: You're 4 minutes and 40 seconds from LOS.
I would like a reconfirmation on your S-band Aux switch in the Downvoice Backup position.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Request you leave it there forever.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston; 70 hours, 56 minutes into the flight.
At this time we would like to play back those historic first words of insertion into lunar orbit as we heard them here at Mission Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The Public Affairs Officer is evidently elated at Apollo 8's achievement and shares the moment with the folk from the media once more.
transcript.speaker: Commentary
